headline: I design the infrastructure
that turns raw Supply Chain data
into boardroom decisions.
hero.sub: 5+ years inside O2C, P2P, Inventory, and International Trade. Not just querying data — building the systems that make it trustworthy.

about.label: // 01 — About
title: The professional who knows
where the data breaks.
about.p1: I'm a Data & Analytics Engineer with over 5 years working inside the most operationally complex domains in business: Order-to-Cash, Procure-to-Pay, Manufacturing, Inventory, and International Trade. Not just analyzing data from them — living inside them.
about.p2: That depth is my edge. I understand how a Purchase Order behaves differently across three ERP systems, why an invoice status field means different things before and after month-end close, and where the data silently breaks before it ever reaches a dashboard.
about.p3: I've participated in system implementations and data transitions for NetSuite, Dynamics AX, Zoho, and OSGT — working as both Project Manager and Key User. That means I design data models with source systems in mind, not against them.
about.p4: My deliverables get used. That's the metric that matters.

projects.label: // 02 — Projects
title: Real dashboards. Real operations.
Built for decisions, not reports.
projects.modal_no_images: Dashboard screenshots will be available after image extraction.

items.title: Import KPI Dashboard — Cost Control & Performance Tracking
items.overview: Centralized Power BI dashboard replacing scattered Excel files and manual AX exports. Gave the import team and directors a single live view of costs, lead times, and channel performance.
items.problem: Import cost data lived in multiple spreadsheets maintained by different team members. There was no unified view of exchange rate impact on landed costs, no visibility into green-channel classification rates, and no way to track storage charges by period without a manual OSGT export. Directors received a monthly PowerPoint — always one month late.
items.architecture: Data Warehouse (SQL Server) fed by scheduled AX exports and OSGT data pulls via Python scripts. Power BI connected directly to the DW views. DAX measures for exchange-rate-adjusted costs, channel index calculation, and storage charge aggregation. Role-level security for team vs. director access.
items.result: Real-time visibility on import costs and lead times — reviewed daily by the import team, weekly by directors. Green-channel index and storage tracking fully automated. The monthly PowerPoint was retired. Directors now open Power BI instead of waiting for the report.

items.title: Import Follow-Up — From Purchase Order to Warehouse Receipt
items.overview: End-to-end tracking dashboard for all active import processes. Every order classified as On Time, Late, or Critical — with drill-down by process stage, responsible team, and aging analysis.
items.problem: Import process status was tracked in a team spreadsheet updated manually. There was no alert system, no aging view, and no way to see which stage was causing delays across all processes simultaneously. The logistics team reacted to problems only after they escalated.
items.architecture: Consolidated model pulling from three sources: AX (purchase orders), OSGT (customs stages and clearance), and WMS (warehouse receipt confirmations). Status classification logic (On Time / Late / Critical) built as calculated columns based on configurable lead time thresholds by supplier and transport mode. Aging calculated from PO creation date per stage. Power BI with bookmarks for team vs. stage view.
items.result: Complete PO-to-delivery visibility for 100% of active import processes. The logistics team identified recurring bottlenecks at the customs clearance stage and renegotiated SLAs with the customs broker. Critical order alerts replaced reactive firefighting.

items.title: Export Follow-Up — Outbound Logistics from Order to Delivery
items.overview: Mirror of the Import Follow-Up built for outbound flows. Tracks every export order from sales order creation through customs export, carrier handoff, international transit, and final delivery or factory return.
items.problem: Export process tracking relied entirely on spreadsheets and individual team knowledge. In-transit quantities were invisible to the commercial team — they couldn't tell customers where their orders were. Factory returns (for repair) had no tracking at all. No unified view existed between AX, OSGT, and the WMS.
items.architecture: Same three-source model as Import Follow-Up but adapted for outbound logic: AX (sales orders and returns), OSGT (export customs clearance), WMS (dispatch and physical output). Export lifecycle states modeled to handle standard delivery and factory-return flows independently. Stage-level timing vs. expected lead times visualized as a Gantt-style breakdown.
items.result: The commercial team gained visibility to answer customer queries in real time. In-transit quantities tracked live. Factory return flows mapped and monitored for the first time. Cross-team alignment between logistics and sales improved measurably in the S&OP cycle.

items.title: Service Level Dashboard — OTIF & OCT by Customer, Region, and BU
items.overview: Single dashboard replacing the monthly OTIF calculation spreadsheet. On Time In Full and Order Cycle Time tracked in real time, segmented by sales modality, customer, region, and business unit — with last-year comparison built in.
items.problem: OTIF was calculated manually every month by extracting from the logistics system and crossing with sales order data in Excel. The process took 2–3 days of analyst time, the result was always retrospective, and there was no way to drill into why OTIF dropped in a specific region or BU. Leadership reviewed stale data in meetings.
items.architecture: Data Warehouse as single source, pulling from the Logistics-OTIF BI module and sales order data. Power BI semantic layer with OTIF and OCT defined as explicit DAX measures — On Time (delivery date vs. committed date), In Full (quantity delivered vs. ordered), OCT split into Order-to-Invoice and Invoice-to-Delivery legs. LY comparison via time intelligence. Segmentation by customer, region, modality, and BU via slicers.
items.result: OTIF and OCT updated daily. Leadership reviews current-month performance in the weekly S&OP meeting instead of last month. The regional logistics team identified a specific carrier causing systematic late deliveries and acted on it within the same month the dashboard launched.

items.title: S&OP & Inventory Dashboard — Demand, Replenishment, and Health
items.overview: Comprehensive dashboard built around the three pillars of S&OP: demand behavior, replenishment efficiency, and inventory health. Unified past, present, and future view — replacing five separate planning spreadsheets.
items.problem: S&OP at Strattner ran on five separate SharePoint spreadsheets and manual AX exports. Demand was analyzed separately from inventory. Replenishment triggers were tracked manually. There was no forecast vs. actual comparison. Each S&OP meeting required a half-day of data preparation — and the data was still inconsistent between departments.
items.architecture: Data Warehouse integrating AX inventory and procurement tables with SharePoint-based planning files (via Power Automate export). Power BI model structured around the three S&OP pillars as explicit pages: Demand (historical analysis and forecast accuracy), Replenishment (purchase order coverage and lead time adherence), Inventory Health (stock levels, days of coverage, excess/shortage flags). Supply chain KPI scorecards on a summary page.
items.result: S&OP meeting preparation time cut from half a day to 15 minutes. The supply chain director uses the dashboard as the single source of truth in every planning meeting. Inventory health flags enabled a targeted excess stock reduction campaign, freeing working capital within the quarter.

items.title: CNX Data Lakehouse — dbt + BigQuery + GCP
items.overview: End-to-end data engineering platform covering the full supply chain data lifecycle. Multi-domain semantic layer serving 10+ business units across O2C, P2P, Inventory, and International Trade.
items.problem: Multiple ERP systems, no unified data layer, and business units each maintaining their own metrics. No governance, no lineage, no single source of truth across domains.
items.architecture: Raw ingestion from multiple ERPs and APIs into BigQuery raw layer. dbt Cloud project with 100+ models across staging → intermediate → mart layers. Full lineage documented. Looker semantic layer with governed metrics. Orchestrated via Airflow on GCP. Infrastructure as code.
items.result: One governed semantic layer serving all business units. Self-service analytics enabled for non-technical stakeholders. Pipeline reliability and data quality enforced through dbt tests at every layer.

process.label: // 03 — Process
title: From raw source
to trusted decision.
process.subtitle: Every project follows the same architecture. Click a node to inspect.

raw.title: RAW SOURCE
raw.desc: ERP tables, API exports, manual spreadsheets, legacy files. The real world is messy. This is the starting point for every project.

transform.title: TRANSFORMATION
transform.desc: Business logic enforced as code. Three explicit layers with contracts between them. Nothing goes to the next layer untested.

impact.title: BUSINESS IMPACT
impact.desc: Semantic layers, dashboards, and metrics with named owners and defined questions. Built for adoption, not just visibility.

items.name: dbt
items.desc: I use dbt to enforce business logic as code: modular models, documented contracts, tested metrics. If a KPI goes to production without a test, it's not ready for production.

items.name: BigQuery / GCP
items.desc: My primary analytical layer. I design schemas with query cost and performance in mind — partitioning, clustering, and incremental models are defaults, not afterthoughts.

items.name: Apache Airflow
items.desc: I build pipelines that are observable by design: explicit dependencies, alerting, documentation baked in. Not pipelines that run until they mysteriously don't.

items.name: Looker / Power BI
items.desc: I build for adoption. A dashboard no one uses is a failed project. Every report I deliver has a defined owner, a defined question, and a defined action it enables.

items.name: SQL & Python
items.desc: SQL is how I think about data. Python is how I automate the parts that shouldn't be manual. I use both where they're strongest, not where they're fashionable.

items.name: ERP Systems
items.desc: NetSuite, Dynamics AX, Zoho, OSGT — I've been inside these as Key User and PM. I know how source data is generated, not just how to query it. That changes everything.

contact.label: // 05 — Let's Talk
contact.title: If your team still reconciles metrics in meetings —
contact.sub: Conflicting KPIs, pipelines nobody trusts, data that means different things to different teams. That's the problem I solve. Let's have a direct conversation.
contact.form_message_placeholder: Tell me about the problem, not the solution. I'll figure out the rest.
contact.form_success: Message received. I'll get back to you within 24h.
